message:
perf(ft-kernel-cpu): pairwise dot product in matmul

Final piece in the pairwise-summation precision arc started in
69fce3d (sum/mean) and continued through cfde2de (norm), 56a4a6f
(var/std), 8ba4154 (sum_dim), and 4334ecb (softmax / log_softmax).

`matmul_tensor_contiguous_f64` (and the f32 sibling) used a naive
triple-loop with sequential dot-product accumulator:

    for inner in 0..k {
        acc += lhs[...] * rhs[...];
    }
    out[...] = acc;

For K > 128 (typical d_model in transformers — 768, 1024, 4096) this
drifts at O(K · ε) per output cell. PyTorch's CPU matmul calls BLAS,
which pairwise-sums the dot product, so we match that contract by
staging the K products into a reusable scratch buffer (one
allocation per matmul call, not per output cell) and pairwise-
summing it.

For K <= 128 the pairwise helper falls through to sequential and
produces a bit-identical result to the prior implementation. For
K > 128 cumulative error tightens from O(K · ε) to O(log K · ε).

F32 sibling updated symmetrically. F32's 24-bit mantissa makes the
gap even larger — a K = 1024 dot product accumulates ~5 mantissa
bits of error sequentially, vs ~3 with pairwise.

Test:
  matmul_tensor_contiguous_pairwise_dot_product_precision_at_large_k
  computes <(1/sqrt(K)) ones, (1/sqrt(K)) ones> with K = 4096
  (analytical truth = 1.0) and locks the result within 1e-14.

Downstream impact: every matmul-driven path (linear layers,
attention, transformer feedforward, embedding gather + project) now
inherits the tighter precision contract. ft-nn 150 + ft-conformance
101 + ft-autograd 314 + ft-api 1091 tests all pass — no regression
on any existing exact-equality assertion (those use K <= 128 which
is bit-identical to the prior path).

Quality gates: 12-crate workspace test sweep — ft-kernel-cpu 312 +
ft-api 1091 + ft-conformance 101 + ft-autograd 314 + ft-nn 150 +
ft-optim 74 + ft-data 103 + ft-serialize 15 + ft-runtime 2 +
ft-core 70 + ft-dispatch 9 + ft-device 6 — all green; clippy + ubs
clean.

Co-Authored-By: Claude Opus 4.7 (1M context) <noreply@anthropic.com>
